title: Disease-modifying interactions between chronic kidney disease and osteoarthritis: a new comorbid mouse model
research_question: To develop a preclinical comorbid model of chronic kidney disease (CKD) and osteoarthritis (OA) and investigate the bidirectional disease-modifying interactions between them.
methods_summary: Male C57BL/6 mice underwent 5/6 nephrectomy (5/6Nx) to induce CKD, with or without destabilisation of medial meniscus (DMM) to induce OA, or sham surgeries.

OA pathology and CKD were assessed 12 weeks post-induction using blinded histology scoring, micro-CT, immunohistochemistry for osteoclast and MMP-13 activity, and serum analysis of bone metabolic markers.

Renal function was evaluated via urine protein, creatinine, blood urea, and histology; subchondral bone morphology was analyzed via micro-CT; joint histopathology was scored for cartilage damage, osteophytes, synovitis, and subchondral bone changes.
dataset: 70 male C57BL/6 mice (8-10 weeks old) divided into four groups: sham, 5/6Nx-sham, DMM alone, and 5/6Nx-DMM.
key_findings: 5/6Nx alone did not induce cartilage pathology but increased synovial MMP-13 expression and subchondral bone osteoclastic activity, reducing subchondral trabecular bone volume and mineral density.
DMM alone induced typical OA pathology (cartilage damage, subchondral bone sclerosis, osteophytes, synovitis) and also caused renal fibrosis and proteinuria.
5/6Nx significantly reduced DMM-induced cartilage pathology and osteophyte formation, associated with reduced subchondral bone volume and density and inhibition of osteoclast activation.
DMM in 5/6Nx mice reduced serum phosphate and calcium levels, indicating altered bone metabolism.
Bidirectional disease-modifying interactions were observed: CKD altered OA progression, and OA affected renal function and morphology.
extracted_insights: This study provides the first preclinical comorbid CKD-OA model, demonstrating that CKD-induced subchondral bone resorption and reduced bone mineral density can protect against OA-related cartilage damage and osteophyte formation, while OA itself can induce renal fibrosis and dysfunction, highlighting complex bidirectional cross-talk between the two diseases.
